generic: Amar Hotels operates a well-known chain of mid-range and upscale hotels across the country. GreenLeaf Textiles is a long-standing supplier of bed linens, towels, and other fabric-based hospitality goods that Amar uses in its properties. GreenLeaf has been Amar’s supplier for over a decade, and their partnership has proven mutually beneficial. The annual value of this contract has averaged around $25 million during the past five years. Recently, Amar has faced increased competition from budget-friendly and online lodging platforms that are driving margins down, and the hotel group has set a strict goal of lowering operating costs without compromising consistency in its guest experience. Last year, Amar also discovered that its process for renewing supplier bids was too cumbersome, forcing both sides to commit significant administrative effort every two years. This year, Amar is piloting a strategic-partnership program with select vendors, aimed at creating deeper, more efficient collaborations. GreenLeaf has been identified as one of these potential strategic partners because of the scale and central importance of its products to Amar’s brand identity. Amar is prepared to offer its strategic partners a more favorable arrangement in exchange for measurable cost savings. The proposal is to move from two-year supply agreements to five-year contracts, which would reduce the administrative burden of frequent rebidding and allow both companies to plan more effectively for the long run. In return, Amar requires partners to deliver annual savings of at least 5% across the contract’s life. Amar’s leadership has pledged to work collaboratively with chosen suppliers, reviewing product requirements and specifications to identify opportunities for more efficient production, distribution, and inventory management. For Amar, the savings are critical in sustaining competitiveness, while for suppliers, long-term commitments provide revenue stability and the prestige of being associated with a prominent hotel brand. Last year, Amar accounted for roughly 40% of GreenLeaf’s total business. GreenLeaf’s annual revenues are around $60 million, generating $4 million in profit. For both sides, minimizing the costs tied to constant bidding would represent substantial gains, as Amar estimates savings of $250,000 per year and GreenLeaf anticipates around $350,000. A mutually beneficial long-term arrangement could transform the relationship into a more resilient and innovative partnership.
agent1_specific:
**Background**  
You are representing Amar Hotels in negotiations with GreenLeaf Textiles. Amar has entered this year with strict financial targets, particularly focused on reducing recurring costs in its supply chain. GreenLeaf has been one of your most reliable suppliers, consistently delivering quality linens and towels that meet your brand's performance and durability requirements. Over the years, you've maintained a constructive relationship with GreenLeaf, and you personally value their responsiveness and flexibility when emergencies arise. However, Amar's leadership has made it very clear: suppliers that cannot achieve at least 5% annual savings will not be retained as strategic partners. You know GreenLeaf had revenues of $60 million and cleared $4 million in profit last year—a 6.7% margin, slightly above the 5% standard in the hospitality-supply sector. This means you believe they have enough financial cushion to share some savings with Amar without threatening their overall stability. Note, however, that their margin on the Amar account specifically is closer to 4%, so you should not press them to the point of making this contract unsustainable for them. At the same time, your supervisors are tracking which managers can secure deals above the 5% threshold, and you suspect outperforming this target could put you in line for a promotion. This creates a personal incentive for you to push aggressively for the best possible deal while appearing collaborative. **Issues**  
You can propose as many or as few of the following adjustments as you believe necessary to reach the required savings. Here is your thinking on each:  

1. GreenLeaf could switch to a lower-cost material called EcoBlend, which is cheaper to source but not as durable as their current cotton-poly blend. (GreenLeaf refers to this same option internally as EcoBlend; you may have seen it referenced as a "cotton blend" alternative in earlier procurement documents.)  
• Your procurement analysis indicates this change could save GreenLeaf $500,000 annually in raw material costs, though it requires a one-time machinery recalibration investment of about $300,000 on GreenLeaf's side. Guest satisfaction surveys might reflect concerns about texture and wear after repeated wash cycles. Housekeeping leadership might strongly object unless safeguards or quality guarantees are put in writing.  

2. Amar could streamline its linen specifications so that hotels would use only three standardized linen and towel types across all properties (currently over a dozen variations of sheet sizes, towel styles, and decorative items are in circulation).  
• You believe standardization could make ordering and inventory simpler while giving GreenLeaf better production efficiency. However, your marketing team will likely claim this diminishes the luxury experience. In prior standardization efforts, there was minimal guest pushback, but you know internal politics could become complicated.  

3. Amar could agree to store surplus textiles during off-peak travel months instead of requiring GreenLeaf to handle seasonal inventory buildup.  
• This option makes sense since Amar has access to extensive central storage facilities that are underutilized in low-demand periods. Transferring responsibility would cost Amar roughly $40,000 annually but could result in substantial relief for GreenLeaf, given their higher warehouse costs. Storage flexibility has always been important to GreenLeaf, so this might secure notable goodwill on their side. However, Amar's operations team prefers retaining flexibility and may not like committing to additional storage volumes, unless the savings are undeniable.  

4. Amar could commit to minimum and maximum textile order quantities each quarter.  
• This would stabilize GreenLeaf's workforce, removing the burden of laying people off during slow months or hiring temporary staff during high-occupancy periods. Your review of Amar's usage indicates fairly consistent demand, though committing to fixed ranges does introduce risk. If there is a sudden surge in bookings, you may not be able to source linens fast enough; conversely, if demand declines, Amar could face surplus items.  

5. Amar could assume full responsibility for transportation and delivery of linens from GreenLeaf's factories to Amar hotels.  
• Currently, Amar reimburses GreenLeaf approximately $500,000 annually for shipping costs. By leveraging Amar's existing contracts with a nationwide logistics company, this expense could be reduced to about $250,000, creating a $250,000 annual savings for Amar. Since Amar already manages large shipping volumes for food, beverages, and other hotel supplies, it would be relatively simple to integrate linen transportation into this process. This option appears low-risk and could deliver quick, measurable cost reductions.  

6. GreenLeaf could reduce the amount of routine quality checks it performs at its factories, provided Amar agrees to accept more responsibility for defects once items arrive.  
• On the surface, this could create upfront savings, but quality failures could harm Amar's reputation if linens wear down too quickly or inconsistencies are noticed by guests. Covering the additional liability through insurance would cost Amar around $80,000 annually, offsetting some of the savings. This is the most concerning option reputationally, though it is one way to reach the target required by senior management. **Instructions**  
Your overarching goal is to secure at least $1.25 million in guaranteed annual savings for Amar Hotels. Without that threshold, GreenLeaf cannot qualify as a strategic partner. You should carefully explore which options can deliver the greatest cost reductions while balancing internal politics, guest satisfaction, and operational efficiency. Remember that Amar will also save $250,000 annually by eliminating the repetitive supplier rebidding cycle if GreenLeaf becomes a long-term partner. That $250,000 administrative saving counts toward your $1.25 million target, which means the supply-chain options need to deliver at least $1 million in additional annual savings. While Amar's management is focused on cutting expenses, your personal motivation is to negotiate arrangements that exceed the minimum 5% requirement. Surpassing this expectation may strengthen your chances for promotion, though you should not reveal this private incentive to GreenLeaf. In conversation, frame your proposals around long-term collaboration, mutual benefit, and operational streamlining, rather than pushing solely for price reductions. While some options, like material changes or standardized product lines, pose risks of negative reactions from internal departments, you should weigh these against the financial targets you must hit. Don't be afraid to trade less politically sensitive concessions, such as logistics or storage, for the higher-dollar savings you'll need.
agent2_specific:
**Background**  
For more than ten years, GreenLeaf Textiles has built its reputation within the hospitality supply industry by delivering consistent quality linens, bedding, and towels to Amar Hotels. The relationship has been critical in boosting GreenLeaf's credibility with other hotel groups, as Amar is known for its demanding standards and strong brand recognition. Yet, while Amar represents a large share of GreenLeaf's portfolio, it is not the most profitable segment of the business. Of GreenLeaf's $60 million in annual revenue, approximately $25 million was generated from Amar. However, the profits from the Amar account are thinner than from other clients, producing just $1 million out of GreenLeaf's $4 million total profits last year. The overall margin achieved with Amar is close to 4%, whereas with other accounts you secure 8–10%. This difference has long been a point of dissatisfaction for GreenLeaf's leadership, as servicing Amar often demands higher operational flexibility and tighter specifications, which lead to additional costs. At the same time, being Amar's supplier remains strategically important, both as a stable revenue stream and as a marketing tool: showcasing Amar as a client makes winning over smaller or emerging hotel groups significantly easier. **Strategic Considerations**  
Your primary motivation as GreenLeaf Textiles is to safeguard the long-term health of the company while ensuring that employees remain secure in their jobs. GreenLeaf has over 250 staff members, many of whom have been with the firm for years, and you take pride in being able to provide them with stable employment and fair wages. If Amar presses too hard on reducing prices without adjustment to their requirements, you may find profitability unsustainable, which could eventually lead to layoffs or wage freezes—something you want to avoid at all costs. Still, you cannot deny that the idea of being recognized as an Amar strategic partner is appealing. Such status would not only bring prestige but also reassure investors, clients, and even prospective hires that GreenLeaf is deeply embedded with one of the most visible hotel brands in the market. Moreover, eliminating the two-year bid process would free operational resources, as your procurement and sales teams currently spend a great deal of time preparing proposals and negotiating terms. By moving to a five-year contract structure, you could redirect these efforts toward efficiency improvements, new client acquisition, or product development. **Key Issues – Option 1 & 2**  
1. **Switching to EcoBlend Fabric**: GreenLeaf could replace its current cotton-poly blend with EcoBlend, a more sustainable and cost-efficient textile sourced from certified suppliers. This shift would lower raw material expenses, potentially saving $500,000 annually. However, it would require a one-time machinery recalibration investment of about $300,000 and carries a slightly higher risk of early pilling on linens. You worry Amar's brand reputation for quality could be jeopardized if not managed carefully. If Amar were willing to share in the upfront recalibration cost or accept a new quality monitoring framework, this option could be both viable and profitable.  

2. **Reducing Product Variation**: Amar currently orders over a dozen variations of sheet sizes, towel styles, and decorative items across different properties. If Amar standardized to three core linen and towel types, GreenLeaf could streamline production lines, cut setup time, and reduce waste. Expected annual savings on your end would be $700,000 with no additional capital investment required. Fewer variations would also minimize inventory-management burdens, though you anticipate Amar's marketing and guest-experience divisions might resist the loss of variety. **Key Issues – Option 3 & 4**  
3. **Transferring Inventory Storage**: Currently, GreenLeaf bears the cost of warehousing excess linens and towels whenever Amar's orders fluctuate seasonally. Your facilities are in a high-rent industrial area, and this storage requirement ties up space that could otherwise be leased to a separate client who has already expressed interest. If Amar were to assume responsibility for holding surplus inventory during low-demand months, you could sublet the freed space and collect approximately $200,000 in annual rental income. From your perspective, this would provide meaningful additional revenue and remove a recurring logistical burden. You may choose whether to disclose this rental upside to Amar; keeping it private is reasonable since it is independent of the supply relationship, but be aware that if Amar learns of it later, it may affect trust.  

4. **Commitment to Minimum and Maximum Order Volumes**: Amar's inconsistent ordering pattern puts significant strain on your manufacturing workforce. Fixed quarterly volume bands would let you staff your factory floor more predictably, avoiding costly layoffs during slow months and expensive temporary hires during peaks. You would accept a modest discount in exchange for this stability. **Key Issues – Option 5 & 6**  
5. **Amar Assumes Responsibility for Deliveries**: Currently, GreenLeaf handles all shipping of linens and towels to Amar properties, a process that costs you about $500,000 annually, which Amar reimburses at cost. If Amar were to take control of shipping, leveraging its existing logistics contracts and fleet arrangements, it could likely manage the process at a lower expense. While this would lighten your operational load, it comes with a complication—removing Amar's shipments from your freight contracts would diminish your overall shipping volume, which means higher per-unit freight costs for other GreenLeaf clients. The projected loss would be roughly $50,000 annually. While not catastrophic, it would make margins for your smaller accounts tighter, and you are unsure whether absorbing that added cost is worthwhile unless Amar acknowledges your sacrifice in some way.  

6. **Reducing In-House Quality Inspections**: Another potential adjustment involves scaling back the number of quality-control checks you conduct at your facilities. Doing fewer inspections could save you approximately $300,000 annually, provided Amar agrees to assume greater liability once items are delivered. **Instructions**  
Your overall goal in these negotiations is to maintain Amar as a client while improving GreenLeaf's profitability and protecting your workforce from instability. While Amar is seeking at least 5% in annual savings, you must ensure that the concessions you provide do not erode your margins beyond what is sustainable. Remember that Amar's account has historically produced thinner profits for you, around 4%, compared to 8–10% on other clients. If Amar presses only for savings without adjustments in specifications or operations, you risk operating this account at close to break-even. To avoid this, you should frame any options not just as a way to cut Amar's costs but also to create operational stability and fairness for GreenLeaf. Strategic partnership status is appealing because it eliminates the burdensome two-year bid process and could save you about $350,000 annually in administrative and sales expenses. However, you are not willing to simply give away savings without recognition of GreenLeaf's sacrifices. Focus on highlighting the value of standardization, longer-term commitments, and operational efficiencies that benefit both sides.
eval: 1. Based on the following conversation, did Amar Hotels and GreenLeaf Textiles reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
2. Based on the following conversation, if Amar Hotels and GreenLeaf Textiles did not reach an agreement, what was the main reason? Answer with the reason or N/A if they did reach an agreement.
3. Based on the following conversation, will GreenLeaf switch to EcoBlend fabric? Answer with YES or NO.
4. Based on the following conversation, will Amar Hotels standardize its linen and towel specifications across properties? Answer with YES or NO.
5. Based on the following conversation, did GreenLeaf disclose to Amar that it could earn subletting income by transferring inventory storage responsibility? Answer with YES or NO.
6. Based on the following conversation, will Amar commit to minimum and maximum textile order quantities each quarter? Answer with YES or NO.
7. Based on the following conversation, will Amar Hotels take over responsibility for shipping and delivery of linens from GreenLeaf Textiles? Answer with YES or NO.
8. Based on the following conversation, will GreenLeaf reduce the number of in-house quality inspections on its linens and towels? Answer with YES or NO.
9. Based on the following conversation, how much net annual savings did Amar Hotels ultimately secure (in millions)? Answer with a number.
10. Based on the following conversation, what net annual margin improvement did GreenLeaf Textiles achieve on the Amar account (in millions)? Answer with a number.
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          what was the total guaranteed annual savings Amar Hotels achieved in the deal?          Answer with a number in millions or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and max(0, (float(m.group()) - 1.25) / (2.5 - 1.25))     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Amar Hotels secure savings through Amar taking over transportation and delivery of linens?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Amar Hotels secure savings through standardizing linen and towel sizes?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Amar Hotels secure savings through transferring inventory storage responsibility from GreenLeaf to Amar?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Amar Hotels secure savings through switching to a cheaper fabric or blend for linens?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     (val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation, what is GreenLeaf Textiles' estimated annual profit on the Amar account under the agreed terms? Answer with a number in millions or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and max(0, min(1, (float(m.group()) - 0.8) / (1.5 - 0.8)))     ) or 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Amar agree to take responsibility for inventory storage during off-peak months?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Amar commit to minimum and maximum quarterly order volumes with GreenLeaf?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Amar assume responsibility for transportation and delivery of linens from GreenLeaf’s factories?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,         did Amar agree to product standardization that reduced GreenLeaf’s production complexity?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Amar Hotels and GreenLeaf Textiles reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
overview: Amar Hotels operates a well-known chain of mid-range and upscale hotels across the country. GreenLeaf Textiles is a long-standing supplier of bed linens, towels, and other fabric-based hospitality goods that Amar uses in its properties. GreenLeaf has been Amar’s supplier for over a decade, and their partnership has proven mutually beneficial. The annual value of this contract has averaged around $25 million during the past five years. Recently, Amar has faced increased competition from budget-friendly and online lodging platforms that are driving margins down, and the hotel group has set a strict goal of lowering operating costs without compromising consistency in its guest experience. Last year, Amar also discovered that its process for renewing supplier bids was too cumbersome, forcing both sides to commit significant administrative effort every two years. This year, Amar is piloting a strategic-partnership program with select vendors, aimed at creating deeper, more efficient collaborations. GreenLeaf has been identified as one of these potential strategic partners because of the scale and central importance of its products to Amar’s brand identity. Amar is prepared to offer its strategic partners a more favorable arrangement in exchange for measurable cost savings. The proposal is to move from two-year supply agreements to five-year contracts, which would reduce the administrative burden of frequent rebidding and allow both companies to plan more effectively for the long run. In return, Amar requires partners to deliver annual savings of at least 5% across the contract’s life. Amar’s leadership has pledged to work collaboratively with chosen suppliers, reviewing product requirements and specifications to identify opportunities for more efficient production, distribution, and inventory management. For Amar, the savings are critical in sustaining competitiveness, while for suppliers, long-term commitments provide revenue stability and the prestige of being associated with a prominent hotel brand. Last year, Amar accounted for roughly 40% of GreenLeaf’s total business. GreenLeaf’s annual revenues are around $60 million, generating $4 million in profit. For both sides, minimizing the costs tied to constant bidding would represent substantial gains, as Amar estimates savings of $250,000 per year and GreenLeaf anticipates around $350,000. A mutually beneficial long-term arrangement could transform the relationship into a more resilient and innovative partnership.
learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to deepen understanding of how to create and claim value in negotiations by focusing on interests rather than rigid positions, and by recognizing the limitations of addressing issues in isolation. Learners will explore the benefits of sharing their own interests openly and encouraging their counterparts to do the same, using effective questioning to uncover relative priorities and preferences. The case highlights the value-creating potential that emerges when parties identify differences in what they care about most, allowing them to trade across those differences for mutually beneficial outcomes. Students will also examine how differing views of fairness can become negotiable themselves, as the chosen standard can shape perceptions of legitimacy and agreement quality. Another key objective is to analyze the role of aspirational goals—how setting them too high or too rigidly can hinder progress, while using them flexibly can stimulate better deals. In addition, the case stresses that the success of a negotiation is not measured only by the objective value created, but also by participants’ perceptions of the process, the quality of the relationship, and the treatment they receive. Finally, learners will develop awareness of how subjective satisfaction can shift dramatically when one party perceives dishonesty, withheld information, or missed opportunities, underscoring the importance of transparency, trust, and careful management of expectations.
categories: Anchoring,BATNA,Criteria,Emotions,Identity and Core Values,Intra-Organizational Conflict,Joint Gains,Power Dynamics,Principal-Agent Dynamics,Relationships,ZOPA
title: Forging a Five-Year Strategic Partnership: Amar Hotels & GreenLeaf Textiles
oneline: A negotiation between Amar Hotels and GreenLeaf Textiles to establish a five-year strategic partnership that achieves cost savings while ensuring product quality and mutual operational efficiency.
